--- SOURCE 1: and re-emerging infectious diseases ---
URL: https://apps.who.int/iris/bitstream/handle/10665/178486/WHA49_6_eng.pdf?isAllowed=y&sequence=1

SUMMARY:
Emerging diseases are defined as those whose incidence in humans has increased in the past two decades or whose incidence threatens to increase

FULL CONTENT:
World Health Organization ^ ^ ^ ^ Organisation mondiale de la Santé FORTY-NINTH WORLD HEALTH ASSEMBLY ДсЩЛ Provisional agenda item 18.2 A49/6 29 February 1996 New, emerging，and re-emerging infectious diseases，and revision of the International Health Regulations The Division of Emerging and other Communicable Diseases Surveillance and Control was established on 1 October 1995 in response to resolution WHA48.13. This report summarizes action taken in preparation for, and realization of, the new programme at WHO headquarters, and other activities in WHO against new, emerging, and re-emerging infectious diseases, in particular, action in response to the outbreaks of yellow fever in Kenya, plague in India and Ebola haemorrhagic fever in Zaire. It also outlines briefly steps taken to implement resolution WHA48.7 on revision and updating of the International Health Regulations. More recent developments will be reported in document A49/6 Add.1. The Health Assembly is invited to note the report. DEFINITIONS 1. Emerging diseases are defined as those whose incidence in humans has increased in the past two decades or whose incidence threatens to increase in the near future. Re-emergence is the reappearance of a known disease after a significant decline in incidence. ESTABLISHMENT OF NEW DIVISION 2. The Division of Emerging and other Communicable Diseases Surveillance and Control was established on 1 October 1995. It takes over a large part of the staff and activities of the former Division of Communicable Diseases, strengthened by programme activities and staff from the Epidemiological Surveillance and Statistical Services unit, and the Division of Diarrhoeal and Acute Respiratory Disease Control. The new Division is committed to working closely with other organizations in the United Nations system, nongovernmental organizations, and specialized agencies. Within WHO, response to epidemics will be coordinated with regional offices and the Division of Emergency and Humanitarian Action, which will coordinate resource mobilization and such aspects of operational support as supplies and personnel, while the new Division provides the technical support and identifies the necessary expertise. A49/1 OBJECTIVE 3. The objective of the new programme is to strengthen national and international capacity in the surveillance, prevention and control of communicable diseases, including timely and effective response to those that represent new, emerging and re-emerging public health problems. To achieve this objective, it will promote: 一 development of national and international infrastructure and allocation of resources to monitor, prevent and control communicable diseases and related health problems, including antibiotic resistance; -applied research on the diagnosis, epidemiology, prevention and control of communicable diseases and emerging health problems. PROGRAMME FORMULATION 4. A series of meetings and consultations held since the beginning of 1995 at headquarters and in regional offices with the support of international experts have resulted in concrete recommendations on necessary measures to improve the response to the threat of emerging communicable diseases, definition of objectives, plan of action, strategies and the structure of the new Division and of regional activities. 5. The recommendations concentrate on four major goals: to strengthen surveillance of infectious diseases; to re-form international or regional infrastructure so as to recognize, report and respond to emerging and re-emerging communicable diseases; to foster applied research; and to enhance the international and regional capacity for infectious disease prevention and control. 6. An internal coordinating group on emerging infectious diseases has been established to ensure that expertise within and outside the Division is harnessed to the Organization's response to new, emerging and re-emerging infectious diseases and to present a unified WHO approach. 7. The second meeting1 on emerging infectious diseases held in Geneva in January 1995 reviewed the broad range of current and planned activities for surveillance and control. Staff from the regional offices participated, and the meeting benefited from international expertise from Africa, the Americas and Asia. A plan for the organization and management of activities against emerging diseases was proposed. 8. The African Region follows a five-year plan for prevention of communicable diseases adopted in 1993， focusing on strengthening epidemiological surveillance and control of epidemics. The targets include the staffing of each WHO office with an experienced and well trained epidemiologist by the end of 1995，and epidemiology training for each health district team in the Region by the end of 1998. 9. A meeting2 with international experts was held in the Regional Office for the Americas in June 1995 to discuss strategies for the prevention and control of new, emerging, and re-emerging infectious diseases. A regional plan of action was prepared to develop regional and subregional approaches and to guide Member States in tackling specific problems. The plan of action was presented to the Regional Committee at its session in September 1995. 1 Report of the Second WHO Meeting on Emerging Infectious Diseases. Geneva, Switzerland, 12-13 January 1995. WHO/CDS/BVI/95.2. 2 New, Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases. Report presented to the PAHO/AMRO Regional Committee, Washington, 25-30 September 1995. CD38/17. A49/1 10. A meeting1 was held in the Regional Office for South-East Asia in March 1995 to review national and international experience in relation to the outbreak of plague in India in 1994. The Regional Office also organized an intercountry meeting2 in August 1995 to review the ecological and epidemiological situation related to, and the factors responsible for, new, emerging, and re-emerging infectious diseases in the Region. The aims were to discuss and formulate strategies and identify interventions so as to recognize and respond to these diseases quickly, and to recommend follow-up action for the implementation of these strategies and interventions by WHO and Member States. 11. Plague and emerging or re-emerging viral diseases were discussed by the Regional Committee for Europe in September 1995. The Regional Office is according high priority to the prevention and control of specific communicable diseases and is following up resolution WHA48.13 and other resolutions in the field of communicable diseases. 12. A meeting on new, emerging, and re-emerging infectious diseases was held in the Eastern Mediterranean Region in November 1995，and equally high priority is being given to prevention and control of communicable diseases in this Region. PLAN OF ACTION 13. Clear objectives and planned activities were developed in October and November 1995 in three areas: (1) office of director (including responsibility for the International Health Regulations and the Weekly Epidemiological Record and the capacity for rapid technical response to epidemics in collaboration with the regional offices, coordinated with the Division of Emergency and Humanitarian Action); (2) disease surveillance and control (training, research, zoonotic/ecological studies and promotion of disease surveillance and control strategies); and (3) public health laboratory support (strengthening of public health laboratories; monitoring of viral and bacterial diseases and antimicrobial resistance; training in immunology; vaccinology; biotechnology and biosafety; and research). The plan of action enters into force in January 1996 under the definitive divisional structure. RESOURCES 14. The regular budget provision for the Division's activities in the 1996-1997 biennium is US$ 1 668 000， of which currently US$ 991 000 can be allocated for the activities in the biennium. WHO RESPONSE TO EMERGENCIES DUE TO EPIDEMICS 15. A meeting3 held in Geneva in September 1995 reviewed experience resulting from international collaboration and WHO interventions against outbreaks of yellow fever in Kenya (1992-1993)，plague in India (1994) and Ebola haemorrhagic fever in Zaire (1995). The broad range of recommendations from this meeting and related regional meetings was fundamental in the establishment of the Division of Emerging and other Communicable Diseases Surveillance and Control and in the formulation of its plan of action. The 1 Report of an interregional meeting on prevention and control of plague. New Delhi, India, 13-16 March 1995. WHO/CDS/BVI/95.4. 2 Intercountry Consultative Meeting on Prevention and Control of New, Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases, New Delhi, 21-25 August 1995. 3 Report of the Meeting to Review Epidemic Preparedness, Epidemic Control and Research in Outbreak Situations, Geneva, 25-27 September 1995. WHO/EMC/95.1. A49/6 recommendations will also guide activities in other WHO programmes in the Organization's response to emergencies caused by disease outbreaks and epidemics. 16. The WHO response in India and Zaire, and particularly the latter, was greatly facilitated through the setting-up of a task force with other units and divisions. The task force met daily to maintain telephone contact with the field team, the WHO representative and the Regional Office for Africa, to share and widely disseminate information, and to plan follow-up action. Such coordination is a model for future response to epidemic emergencies. Yellow fever, Kenya 17. Kenya is situated in the yellow-fever endemic zone, i.e., where vectors and animal reservoirs are present. Cases of yellow fever were reported in 1992，for the first time since the 1940s, in an outbreak occurring in Kerio Valley, north-west of Nairobi. Further cases have subsequently been detected in the same area. 18. The WHO response to this outbreak was to provide confirmation of the outbreak in collaborating centres; to support epidemic control by the provision of vaccine; to give support to Kenya's laboratories and train staff; to provide experts to contribute to ecological studies; and to facilitate vaccine research in the area, with seroconversion among HIV-positive children, and monitoring of adverse reactions to yellow fever vaccination in HIV-infected adults. These measures were followed by further extensive laboratory training in Kenya and a diagnostic workshop for virologists from other African countries at risk of yellow fever. Active surveillance led to the recognition of continued low-level transmission of yellow fever. Yellow-fever vaccine has now been incorporated in the Expanded Programme on Immunization in areas at risk. Plague, India 19. Plague re-emerged in India in 1994 for the first time since 1967. An outbreak of bubonic cases in Beed district of Maharashtra State in August 1994 was soon followed by the appearance of cases of pneumonic plague in Surat City in Gujarat State. The outbreak in Surat caused wide concern locally and internationally, leading to the imposition of travel and trade restrictions by a number of countries. 20； WHO collaboration in response to the plague outbreak in India involved confirmation of the epidemic through collaborating centres; epidemic control; measures to facilitate ecological studies of rats and improve laboratory facilities; and coordination of information in the form of press releases. Ebola haemorrhagic fever, Zaire 21. In early May 1995 WHO was notified of an outbreak of haemorrhagic fever in Zaire subsequently confirmed as Ebola haemorrhagic fever. Two days later a team of international experts, including WHO staff, was on site, implementing surveillance, prevention and control activities. The outbreak was brought under control and was declared over on 24 August 1995 after two incubation periods. From the first case, traced to early January 1995，to the discharge of the last on 14 July 1995，a total of 316 cases occurred of which 245 were fatal (77%). 22. WHO collaboration in this outbreak was extensive: a collaborating centre confirmed the diagnosis and epidemic control was facilitated by the coordination of logistics (transport, supplies and equipment) for the international response, by donor mobilization, and by the issue of daily press releases. It facilitated the participation of international experts who contributed to the response, and helped to fill gaps in assistance by providing funds locally for training and other activities necessary for epidemic control. A49/6 23. WHO, through collaborating centres and through the work of other international experts, facilitated other epidemiological and clinical studies, giving direct support where necessary to ensure that the appropriate expertise could be assembled at the site of the epidemic; WHO collaborating centres were also involved in ecological studies seeking a natural reservoir of the virus. Finally, WHO facilitated the establishment of a medium-term infrastructure strengthened by teams and trainees after the outbreak had been brought under control, and in collaboration with international experts from various countries. INTERNATIONAL HEALTH REGULATIONS 24. A consultation on the international response to epidemics and application of the International Health Regulations was held in Geneva (11-14 December 1995) to consider the continuous evolution in the public health threat caused by many infectious diseases, the likelihood that the considerable increase in international travel will disseminate diseases rapidly, and the need for, or role of, the International Health Regulations in the twenty-first century. The recommendations of the consultation will be considered by the Committee on International Surveillance of Communicable Diseases1 when it deliberates on the revision of the Regulations. The proposed revised International Health Regulations will be presented to a forthcoming Health Assembly for decision. MATTERS FOR THE PARTICULAR ATTENTION OF THE HEALTH ASSEMBLY 25. The Health Assembly is invited to note the report. 1 This Committee (until 1969 the Committee on International Quarantine) brings together members appointed by the Director-General, who is its Secretary, from appropriate WHO expert advisory panels for the purposes outlined in its Regulations (see Official Records, 1974, No. 217, pp. 73-79，and 1977，No. 240, p. 62, later reproduced in document HST/CISCD/86.1); in particular, to review the application of and submit recommendations on the International Health Regulations and related matters. o r u ( ^ Ш ) World Health Organization Organisation mondiale de la Santé FORTY-NINTH WORLD HEALTH ASSEMBLY Provisional agenda item 18.2 A49/6 Add.1 16 May 1996 New, emerging，and re-emerging infectious diseases，and revision of the International Health Regulations This addendum to document A49/6 provides a summary of activities since February 1996. It reports on programme planning and management; support for national programmes for surveillance and control of communicable diseases; progress in implementation of resolution WHA48.7 calling for revision of the International Health Regulations; and the response to recent outbreaks and epidemics. PROGRAMME PLANNING AND MANAGEMENT 1 • The draft strategic plan for the period 1996-2000 was reviewed at an international consultation in February 1996. In accordance with the recommendations of the consultation the plan has been modified, notably giving greater emphasis to normative functions in the surveillance and control of communicable diseases. The organizational structure of the Division of Emerging and other Communicable Diseases Surveillance and Control was modified accordingly and has now been finalized. DISEASE SURVEILLANCE AND CONTROL 2. Work is closely coordinated at headquarters and in the regional offices to strengthen and support national programmes for disease surveillance, prevention and control. Specific recent activities include: • evaluation of the national system of epidemiological surveillance in Benin, and planning for a more efficient and sustainable system; • evaluation of health information systems in Uganda, and development of a plan for nation-wide implementation including a health information policy and health monitoring framework; • development of a national programme for cholera control in Cape Verde, with a series of workshops for increasing political commitment and strengthening of preparedness and laboratory capability to deal with epidemics; • decision on the composition of influenza vaccine for the 1996/97 season, based on data gathered and analysed in ihe WHO influenza surveillance system; A49/6 Add. 1 • preparation of strategies for planning to prevent an influenza pandemic in view of an anticipated major shift in antigenicity during coming years; • establishment of a global database and network for monitoring antimicrobial resistance, and development of laboratory training materials and a quality assessment system; • training in laboratory diagnosis of yellow fever and preparedness to deal with epidemics in East Africa; • cosponsorshipof an international consultation to establish priorities for surveillance and public health control of hepatitis C, and establishment of a surveillance database; • initiation of discussions with biosafety experts and I ATA on the development of improved regulations for the transport of infectious materials; • preparation of recommendations to limit the spread of bovine spongiform encephalopathy and to minimize the risk of human infection; • establishment of surveillance for variant Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease; • establishment of a regional network for the surveillance and control of emerging hantavirus infections in South America; • preparation of disease-specific guidelines and fact sheets; • rapid dissemination of communicable disease news and epidemiological information; establishment of a "home page" for the Division on the World-Wide Web, and electronic distribution of the Weekly Epidemiological Record. INTERNATIONAL HEALTH REGULATIONS 3. In the follow-up to resolution WHA48.7, which requests the Director-General to take steps to prepare a revision of the International Health Regulations, WHO held an informal consultation from 11 to 14 December 1995 to consider the international response to recent epidemics and the application of the International Health Regulations. The consultation noted that changes in the world health situation and the increase in international travel are also important factors in the application of the Regulations. 4. The consultation concluded that the Regulations have adequately served, and continue to serve, the principles on which they were conceived. However, they fail to regulate procedures necessary for the management of new and re-emerging diseases, especially those which are an international threat. Global coordination is still vitally needed for such purposes as monitoring, reporting and response. The experts considered that the international control of infectious diseases is more effective if undertaken through improved surveillance and intervention strategies than if quarantine measures are applied at sites distant from the source of infection. 5. The consultation therefore stressed that maximum security against the international spread of diseases involving minimum interference with world traffic and trade should remain the basic principle of the revised International Health Regulations. A49/6 Add. 1 6. The consultation made the following key recommendations: (i) the Regulations should be revised and expanded; in particular, the current practice of immediate reporting of only three specific diseases should be replaced by immediate reporting to WHO of defined syndromes representing the occurrence of diseases of international importance; emphasis should be placed on reporting of all basic epidemiological information that will be useful in disease control measures; (ii) the revised Regulations should be accompanied by a practical handbook defining criteria for international reporting, and facilitating the use of the Regulations; (iii) application of the revised Regulations should be integrated into all epidemic surveillance and control activities at global, regional and national levels; (iv) the Regulations should be expanded to include a description of inappropriate or unnecessary interventions and should provide clear indications as to why these actions are not required. 7. These recommendations have been sent by the Director-General to Member States for comment in accordance with operative paragraph I of resolution WHA48.7, which urges Member States to participate in the revision of the Regulations. 8. It is anticipated that the Director-General will convene a meeting of the Committee on International Surveillance of Communicable Diseases to consider, in the light of the conclusions and recommendations of the informal consultation and the comments from Member States, how the Regulations should be revised. The proposed revision resulting from the Committee's deliberations will be submitted to the Health Assembly for adoption under Article 21 of the WHO Constitution. RESPONSE TO OUTBREAKS AND EPIDEMICS 9. WHO (the Division of Emerging and other Communicable Diseases Surveillance Division of Emergency and Humanitarian Action, and regional offices) has responded outbreaks and epidemics. Ebola haemorrhagic fever, Gabon 10. An outbreak of Ebola haemorrhagic fever occurred in a village in Ogoué-Ivindo Province, Gabon, between January and March 1996. It was linked to the butchering, transport and preparation for consumption of a chimpanzee found dead in a forest. A total of 37 cases occurred, of whom 21 died; 21 (19 of whom died) of the 37 had been directly involved in the contacts with the dead chimpanzee. Rapid identification of the disease and appropriate control measures quickly brought the outbreak under control. It was officially declared over on 23 April 1996. 11. The WHO response comprised confirmation of the diagnosis in a WHO collaborating centre and investigation and control of the outbreak by a team with staff from the Division of Emerging and other Communicable Diseases Surveillance and Control and the Regional Office for Africa, including tracing of patients and contacts, training of local health staff and establishment of a post-epidemic surveillance system in Gabon in collaboration with national and international partners. and Control, the to the following A49/6 Add. 1 Yellow fever, Liberia 12. An outbreak of yellow fever in and around the port city of Buchanan in Grand Bassa County, Liberia, reached a peak in November-December 1995, with a total of 356 reported cases and nine deaths. The outbreak was brought under control by a mass vaccination campaign and destruction of mosquito breeding sites. More than 80% of the target population of over one million people were vaccinated. The outbreak being thus contained, no cases occurred in the capital, Monrovia, or in neighbouring countries. 13. The WHO response included epidemic investigation by a team from the Regional Office for Africa, confirmation of diagnosis, mobilization of resources (involving the Division of Emergency and Humanitarian Action at headquarters) and leadership of the vaccination campaign and vector control activities. Meningitis，West Africa 14. Major epidemics of meningitis occurred this year in 11 countries in Africa. Over 38 000 cases were reported with over 5000 deaths, mainly in Burkina Faso, Chad, Mali, Niger and Nigeria. The epidemics, which have not yet ended, are due to Neisseria meningitidis serogroup A. Control of epidemics depends on rapid diagnosis and treatment of cases and vaccination of susceptible population groups. The situation is being brought under control, although shortage of vaccine has hampered the activities, and it is expected that the epidemics will only come to a complete end with the onset of the rainy season. 15. The WHO response included an evaluation of the epidemics by a team of staff of the Division and the Regional Office for Africa; confirmation of diagnosis in a WHO collaborating centre; mobilization of resources to purchase antibiotics and vaccine (involving the Division of Emergency and Humanitarian Action); organization of vaccination programmes; and training activities. Transmissible spongiform encephalopathies 16. In the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland, where a major epidemic of bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE) began in the late 1980s, 10 cases of atypical Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease (CJD) have been recognized recently in young adults. The possibility of a causal link between this syndrome and BSE has been proposed as an explanation of the new human disease but there is no direct evidence at present. 17. WHO has previously held three consultations on transmissible spongiform encephalopathies (1991， 1993, 1995). Following the recent British findings, two further international consultations have been held in 1996 to review the available scientific information on transmissible spongiform encephalopathies, to make recommendations on measures to limit the spread of BSE, to prevent as completely as possible any human exposure to the BSE agent, and to draw up priorities for further research on this group of diseases. A global surveillance system for variant CJD (V-CJD) has been set up through regional offices. MATTERS FOR THE PARTICULAR ATTENTION OF THE HEALTH ASSEMBLY 18. The Health Assembly may wish to note the report.

--- SOURCE 2: GUIDANCE FOR THE INTERPRETATION OF THE OIE ... ---
URL: https://www.woah.org/app/uploads/2021/06/a-guidance-tc-emergingdiseases-20210304.pdf

SUMMARY:
The definition of emerging disease only applies to diseases not already included in the OIE List. AVAILABILITY AND QUALITY OF INFORMATION. Before undertaking

FULL CONTENT:
March 2021 Guidance for the interpretation of the OIE Terrestrial Code definition of emerging disease 1 GUIDANCE FOR THE INTERPRETATION OF THE OIE TERRESTRIAL CODE DEFINITION OF EMERGING DISEASE Guidance for the interpretation of the OIE Terrestrial Code definition of emerging disease This guidance document is intended to be used by OIE-selected experts when undertaking an assessment to determine if an infectious disease meets the OIE definition of emerging disease as described in the Glossary of the Terrestrial Code. The definition of emerging disease only applies to diseases not already included in the OIE List. AVAILABILITY AND QUALITY OF INFORMATION Before undertaking the assessment, one should assess if the strength of the available scientific evidence is sufficient for taking an informed decision. To be sufficient, the available scientific information should provide evidence on the susceptibility, virulence, and impact in the animal host and epidemiological findings on the potential role it could play in the disease epidemiology, including on disease transmission and distribution and impact on human or animal health. Information should come from a reliable source. CURRENT DEFINITION OF EMERGING DISEASE According to the Glossary of the Terrestrial Code, emerging disease: ‘means a new occurrence in an animal of a disease, infection or infestation, causing a significant impact on animal or public health resulting from: • a change of a known pathogenic agent or its spread to a new geographical area or species; or • a previously unrecognised pathogenic agent or disease diagnosed for the first time.’ Below is some guidance as to the interpretation for some key terms used in the definition (shown bolded in the definition above). • Disease, infection or infestation: Diseases, infections, or infestations that are not associated with a transmissible agent are out of the scope for the Terrestrial Code and for this current guidance. To have this condition met, the infectious aetiology of the disease should be proven. • Significant impact: In general, the significant impact should take into account the direct impact of the new occurrence of a disease, infection, or infestation in animals, or its consequential impact on public health. 2 Guidance for the interpretation of the OIE Terrestrial Code definition of emerging disease An impact can be defined ‘significant’ if at least one of the following conditions is met: − Natural transmission from animals to humans is suspected, and human infection is associated with severe consequences. The condition should be considered met if there is clear scientific evidence1 that the pathogenic agent is zoonotic, and that the disease has severe consequences in humans. The public health impact of the disease should be taken into consideration at the population, and not at only at the individual, level (e.g., according to WHO-DALYs). One-off occurrence of disease in humans is not sufficient to consider the condition as met; − The disease has been shown to have a significant impact on the health of domestic animals at the level of a country or a zone taking into account the occurrence and severity of the clinical signs, including significant morbidity, mortality and/or associated direct production losses. The condition should be considered met if there is evidence1 that the pathogenic agent is the causal agent of the mortality or morbidity (including associated loss of production and taking into account issues such as impaired fertility), or productivity impacts such as a reduction in the quantity or quality of animal products from susceptible species. Indirect losses due to disease control activities, environmental impact (e.g., carcase disposal, disinfection) or trade restrictions should not be considered when assessing the impact; − The disease has been shown to, or scientific evidence indicates that it would, have a significant impact on the health of wildlife taking into account the occurrence and severity of the clinical signs, including direct economic losses and mortality, and any threat to the viability of a wildlife population. The condition should be considered met if there is evidence1 of actual or potential mortality or morbidity in susceptible wildlife populations and/or negative impacts on the biodiversity of wildlife associated with the disease being assessed. Note: wildlife populations include feral animals, captive wild animals, and wild animals. Thus, they can include populations that are commercially farmed (captive wild animals) and hence are an economic asset. However, consideration should also be given to impacts on populations that have ecological or environmental significance. For example, the involvement of endangered species, or mortalities in wildlife populations that could result in significant ecological disruption. • Change of a known pathogenic agent: the change of a known non-OIE-listed pathogenic agent should be interpreted as a change in the epidemiological behaviour (e.g., occurrence in a new species or change of host species range, change of transmission pathways, or unexpected increased morbidity or mortality) of the pathogenic agent itself, or the appearance of a new strain of a pathogenic agent having a significant impact on animal or public health. • New geographical area: the spread of a non-OIE-listed pathogenic agent to a new geographical area should be considered as the spread to a geographical region, where the disease has never been reported before, and the entry and transmission of the pathogenic agent should have been proven associated with international movement of animals (including natural and non-commercial movements, such as migrations or transhumance) or their products (and associated movement of fomites, including people), or vectors. ______________ 1 The provision of evidence is defined as follows: ‘peer-reviewed publications, official reports, grey literature or science-based evidence provided by subject-matter experts’

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Infectious disease of emerging pathogen, often novel in its outbreak range or transmission mode

For the medical journal, see [Emerging Infectious Diseases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_Infectious_Diseases "Emerging Infectious Diseases").

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Global_Examples_of_Emerging_and_Re-Emerging_Infectious_Diseases.jpg/330px-Global_Examples_of_Emerging_and_Re-Emerging_Infectious_Diseases.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Global_Examples_of_Emerging_and_Re-Emerging_Infectious_Diseases.jpg)

When [Anthony Fauci](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anthony_Fauci "Anthony Fauci") became director of the [NIAID](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NIAID "NIAID"), he drew a map of the world for presentation at a congressional hearing that showed a single notable emerging infectious disease threat: HIV. Since then, he has continually updated the map, now showing the emergence of numerous infectious disease threats to illustrate the experiences of his years in office as well as highlighting certain infections that had emerged before HIV.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-1)

An **emerging infectious disease** (**EID**) refer to infectious diseases that have either newly appeared in a population or have existed but are rapidly increasing in incidence, geographic range, or severity due to factors such as environmental changes, antimicrobial resistance, and human-animal interactions.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-NIOSH-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-3) The minority that are capable of developing [efficient transmission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virulence "Virulence") between humans can become major [public](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public_health "Public health") and [global](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_health "Global health") concerns as potential causes of [epidemics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Epidemic "Epidemic") or [pandemics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pandemic "Pandemic").[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Woolhouse2005-4) Their many impacts can be [economic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Economic "Economic") and [societal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Societal "Societal"), as well as clinical.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Morens2013-5) EIDs have been increasing steadily since at least 1940.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-6)

For every decade since 1940, there has been a consistent increase in the number of EID events from wildlife-related [zoonosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zoonosis "Zoonosis"). Human activity is the primary driver of this increase, with [loss of biodiversity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loss_of_biodiversity "Loss of biodiversity") a leading mechanism.[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-7)

Emerging infections account for at least 12% of all human [pathogens](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pathogen "Pathogen").[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-8) EIDs can be caused by newly identified [microbes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Microbe "Microbe"), including [novel](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novel_virus "Novel virus") species or strains of [virus](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Virus "Virus")[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-9) (e.g. [novel coronaviruses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novel_coronavirus "Novel coronavirus"), [ebolaviruses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ebolavirus "Ebolavirus"), [HIV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HIV "HIV")). Some EIDs [evolve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") from a known pathogen, as occurs with new strains of [influenza](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Influenza "Influenza"). EIDs may also result from spread of an existing disease to a new population in a different geographic region, as occurs with [West Nile fever outbreaks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_West_Nile_virus_outbreaks "List of West Nile virus outbreaks"). Some known diseases can also emerge in areas undergoing ecologic transformation (as in the case of [Lyme disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lyme_disease "Lyme disease")[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Kilpatrick2017-10)). Others can experience a resurgence as a **re-emerging infectious disease**, like [tuberculosis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis")[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Porta2014-11) (following [drug resistance](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Drug_resistance "Drug resistance")) or [measles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Measles "Measles").[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Fraser-bell2019-12) Nosocomial (hospital-acquired) infections, such as [methicillin-resistant _Staphylococcus aureus_](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Methicillin-resistant_Staphylococcus_aureus "Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus") are emerging in hospitals, and are extremely problematic in that they are resistant to many antibiotics.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-13) Of growing concern are adverse synergistic interactions between emerging diseases and other infectious and [non-infectious conditions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Non-communicable_diseases "Non-communicable diseases") leading to the development of novel [syndemics](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Syndemic "Syndemic").

Many EID are [zoonotic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zoonotic "Zoonotic"),[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Woolhouse2005-4) deriving from pathogens present in animals, with only occasional [cross-species transmission](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cross-species_transmission "Cross-species transmission") into human populations.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-ACAIM-WACEM_COVID-19_Consensus_Paper-14) For instance, most [emergent viruses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emergent_virus "Emergent virus") are zoonotic[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Woolhouse2005-4) (whereas other [novel viruses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Novel_virus "Novel virus") may have been circulating in the species without being recognized, as occurred with [hepatitis C](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hepatitis_C "Hepatitis C")[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Houghton2009-15)).

History of the concept of emerging infectious diseases
------------------------------------------------------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Emerging_infectious_disease&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History of the concept of emerging infectious diseases")]

The French doctor Charles Anglada (1809–1878) wrote a book in 1869 on extinct and new diseases.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-16) He did not distinguish infectious diseases from others (he uses the terms reactive and affective diseases, to mean diseases with an external or internal cause, more or less meaning diseases with or without an observable external cause). He writes in the introduction:

> A widely held opinion among physicians admits the invariability of pathologies. All the illnesses which have existed or which have an outbreak around us are categorized according to arrested and preconceived types, and must enter one way or the other into the frameworks established by the nosologists. History and observation protest wildly against this prejudice, and this is what they teach: Diseases which have disappeared and whose traces are confined to the archives of science, are followed by other diseases, unknown to the contemporary generation, and which come for the first time to assert their rights. In other words, there are _extinct_ and _new_ diseases.

[Charles Nicolle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charles_Nicolle "Charles Nicolle"), laureate of the [Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine") elaborated the concept of emergence of diseases in his 1930 book _[Naissance, vie et mort des maladies infectieuses](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/s:Naissance,\_vie\_et\_mort\_des\_maladies\_infectieuses "fr:s:Naissance, vie et mort des maladies infectieuses")_ (Birth, Life and Death of Infectious Diseases), and later in [_Destin des maladies infectieuses_](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Destin_des_maladies_infectieuses "fr:Destin des maladies infectieuses") (Fate of Infectious Diseases)[[17]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-17) published in 1933 which served as lecture notes for his teaching of a second year course at the [Collège de France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Coll%C3%A8ge_de_France "Collège de France"). In the introduction of the book he sets out the program of the lectures:

> It is this historical existence, this _destiny_ that will be the subject of our talks. I will have to answer, to the extent that our current knowledge allows, questions that you have asked yourself, that every thoughtful or simply curious mind asks: have the infectious diseases that we observe today always existed? Or have some of them appeared in the course of history? Can we assume that new ones will appear? Can we assume that some of these diseases will disappear? Have some of them already disappeared? Finally, what will become of humanity and domestic animals if, as a result of more and more frequent contacts between people, the number of infectious diseases continues to increase?

The term emerging disease has been in use in scientific publications since the beginning of the 1960s at least[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Ndow-2019-18) and is used in the modern sense by [David Sencer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/David_Sencer "David Sencer") in his 1971 article "Emerging Diseases of Man and Animals"[[19]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-19) where in the first sentence of the introduction he implicitly defines emerging diseases as "infectious diseases of man and animals currently emerging as public health problems" and as a consequence also includes re-emerging diseases:

> Infectious diseases of man and animals currently emerging as public health problems include some old acquaintances and some that are new in respect to identity or concept.

He also notes that some infectious agents are newly considered as diseases because of changing medical technologies:

> But there are also many familiar organisms formerly considered nonpathogenic that are now associated with nosocomial infections, use of artificial kidneys, and the acceptance or rejection of organ transplants, for example.

He concludes the introduction with a word of caution:

> And so infectious disease, one of man's oldest enemies, survives as an adversary that calls forth our best efforts.

However, to many people in the 1960s and 1970s the emergence of new diseases appeared as a marginal problem, as illustrated by the introduction to the 1962 edition of Natural History of Infectious Disease by [Macfarlane Burnet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macfarlane_Burnet "Macfarlane Burnet"):[[20]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-20)

> to write about infectious disease is almost to write of something that has passed into history

as well as the epilogue of the 1972 edition:[[21]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-21)

> On the basis of what has happened in the last thirty years, can we forecast any likely developments for the 1970s? If for the present we retain a basic optimism and assume no major catastrophes occur [...] the most likely forecast about the future of infectious disease is that it will be very dull. There may be some wholly unexpected emergence of a new and dangerous infectious disease, but nothing of the sort has marked the past fifty years.

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Trends_in_Infectious_Diseases_Mortality%2C_1900-1996.png/250px-Trends_in_Infectious_Diseases_Mortality%2C_1900-1996.png)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Trends_in_Infectious_Diseases_Mortality,_1900-1996.png)

Throughout the 20th century until 1980, with the exception of the 1918 Spanish flu pandemic, the death rate from infectious diseases in the United States was steadily decreasing. However, because of the AIDS epidemic, the death rate from infectious diseases increased by 58% between 1980 and 1992.

The concept gained more interest at the end of the 1980s as a reaction to the [AIDS epidemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_HIV/AIDS "History of HIV/AIDS"). On the side of epistemology, [Mirko Grmek](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mirko_Grmek "Mirko Grmek") worked on the concept of emerging diseases while writing his book on the history of AIDS[[22]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-22) and later in 1993 published an article[[23]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-23) about the concept of emerging disease as a more precise notion than the term "new disease" that was mostly used in France at that time to qualify AIDS among others.

Also under the shock of the emergence of AIDS, epidemiologists wanted to take a more active approach to anticipate and prevent the emergence of new diseases. [Stephen S. Morse](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stephen_S._Morse "Stephen S. Morse") from [The Rockefeller University](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Rockefeller_University "The Rockefeller University") in New York was chair and principal organizer of the [NIAID](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NIAID "NIAID")/[NIH](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/National_Institutes_of_Health "National Institutes of Health") Conference "Emerging Viruses: The Evolution of Viruses and Viral Diseases" held 1–3 May 1989 in Washington, DC. In the article summarizing the conference the authors write:[[24]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-24)

> Challenged by the sudden appearance of AIDS as a major public health crisis [...] jointly sponsored the conference "Emerging Viruses: The Evolution of Viruses and Viral Diseases" [...] It was convened to consider the mechanisms of viral emergence and possible strategies for anticipating, detecting, and preventing the emergence of new viral diseases in the future.

They further note:

> Surprisingly, most emergent viruses are zoonotic, with natural animal reservoirs a more frequent source of new viruses than is the sudden evolution of a new entity. The most frequent factor in emergence is human behavior that increases the probability of transfer of viruses from their endogenous animal hosts to man.

In a 1991 paper[[25]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-25) Morse underlines how the emergence of new infectious diseases (of which the public became aware through the AIDS epidemic) is the opposite of the then generally expected _retreat_ of these diseases:

> The striking successes achieved with antibiotics, together with widespread application of vaccines for many previously feared viral diseases, made it appear to many physicians and the public that infectious diseases were retreating and would in time be fully conquered. Although this view was disputed by virologists and many specialists in infectious diseases, it had become a commonplace to suggest that infectious diseases were about to become a thing of the past [...].

As a direct consequence of the 1989 conference on emerging viruses, the [Institute Of Medicine](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Institute_Of_Medicine "Institute Of Medicine") convened in February 1991 the 19-member multidisciplinary Committee on Emerging Microbial Threats to Health, co-chaired by [Joshua Lederberg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joshua_Lederberg "Joshua Lederberg") and [Robert Shope](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Robert_Shope "Robert Shope"), to conduct an 18-month study. According to the report produced by the committee in 1992,[[26]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Institute-of-Medicine-(US)-Committee-on-Emerging-Microbial-Threats-to-Health-1992-26) its charge "was to identify significant emerging infectious diseases, determine what might be done to deal with them, and recommend how similar future threats might be confronted to lessen their impact on public health." The report recommended setting up a surveillance program to recognize emerging diseases and proposed methods of intervention in case an emergent disease was discovered.

> A well-designed, well-implemented surveillance program can detect unusual clusters of disease, document the geographic and demographic spread of an outbreak, and estimate the magnitude of the problem. It can also help to describe the natural history of a disease, identify factors responsible for emergence, facilitate laboratory and epidemiological research, and assess the success of specific intervention efforts.

The proposed interventions were based on the following: the U.S. public health system, research and training, vaccine and drug development, vector control, public education and behavioral change. A few years after the 1989 Emerging Viruses conference and the 1992 IOM report, the [Program for Monitoring Emerging Diseases](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Program_for_Monitoring_Emerging_Diseases "Program for Monitoring Emerging Diseases") (ProMED) was formed by a group of scientists as a follow-up in 1994[[27]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-27) and the Centres for Disease Control (CDC) launched the [_Emerging Infectious Diseases_ journal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_Infectious_Diseases_(journal) "Emerging Infectious Diseases (journal)") in 1995.[[18]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-Ndow-2019-18)

A decade later the IOM convened the Committee on Emerging Microbial Threats to Health in the 21st Century which published its conclusions in 2003.[[28]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-National-Academies-Press-2003-28)

In April 2000 the WHO organized a meeting on Global Outbreak Alert and Response,[[29]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emerging_infectious_disease#cite_note-29) which was the founding act of the [Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Global_Outbreak_Alert_and_Response_Network "Global Outbreak Alert and Response Network").

In 2014, the [Western African Ebola virus epidemic](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Western_African_

URL: https://www1.paho.org/english/gov/cd/cd41_16.pdf

SUMMARY:
Reemerging diseases are those conditions that have reappeared after a significant decrease in incidence.

DESCRIPTION: A presentation slide lists four criteria for defining emerging diseases: newly emerging diseases, re-emerging diseases, new manifestations of known disease agents, and the introduction of known agents to new territories.

URL: https://image1.slideserve.com/1799910/causes-of-emerging-diseases-l.jpg
DESCRIPTION: Several emerging diseases such as Ebola, Zika virus, and SARS are depicted across different regions on a world map, illustrating their geographic origins and spread.

URL: https://image.slidesharecdn.com/emerginginfectiousdiseases-paper203-160917055818/95/emerging-infectious-diseases-paper-203-5-638.jpg?cb=1474092724
DESCRIPTION: The image displays a slide defining emerging infectious diseases, highlighting that they are diseases of infectious origin with increased incidence in humans or recent emergence, including re-emerging diseases and previously unknown infectious agents causing public health problems.

DESCRIPTION: The diagram illustrates the interplay of human factors, viral factors, and environmental and ecological factors contributing to the emergence or re-emergence of infectious viral diseases, highlighting elements like globalization, urbanization, poor sanitation, and climate change.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Emerging diseases are defined by increased incidence or threat in the past two decades, often due to environmental changes or pathogen adaptation.
Accurate classification of emerging diseases aids in effective surveillance, response strategies, and resource allocation, ensuring timely public health interventions and outbreak control.
Challenges in identifying cases include lack of awareness, limited diagnostic resources, and overlapping symptoms with other diseases, complicating timely recognition and response.
